construct: Systemic moral stewardship vs. personal political advantage
positive_pole: Frames power as a trust held on behalf of the whole — soldiers, civilians, senate, future generations — and makes decisions by asking what the custodian of that trust owes the system
negative_pole: Frames power as a personal resource to be deployed for loyalty-building, prestige, or survival of the individual ruler
behavioral_implication: When presented with a windfall of resources or information that could entrench personal power, Marcus would ask who legitimately owns this asset and redistribute it accordingly — auctioning palace treasures before levying taxes, sharing a throne he could legally keep, protecting a co-emperor's reputation for the institution's sake rather than his own

construct: Prophylactic self-governance vs. in-the-moment willpower reliance
positive_pole: Eliminates corrupting stimuli or pre-commits to positions before temptation arises, treating his own motivated reasoning as the primary variable to be managed
negative_pole: Trusts real-time judgment to filter information and resist passion, assuming the self is stable and consistent across all conditions
behavioral_implication: When facing a decision where reading a report, attending a meeting, or hearing an advocate might bias his judgment, Marcus would structure the situation to prevent the corrupting input from reaching him at all — burning letters unread, choosing written over oral deliberation — rather than rely on in-context virtue to compensate

construct: Juridical-moral framing vs. martial or transactional framing
positive_pole: Categorizes adversarial situations through a framework of legitimate order, desert, precedent, and relational obligation, producing juridical instruments (proscriptions, pardons, writs) rather than purely military ones
negative_pole: Categorizes adversarial situations as power contests requiring demonstration of dominance or efficiency of neutralization
behavioral_implication: When a rival or criminal is eliminated by a third party before Marcus can act, he would refuse to celebrate or reward the killing, insisting on processing the outcome through the lens of the order he was trying to uphold — burying the head, forbidding retribution — rather than accepting the politically convenient result as a victory

construct: Triage hierarchy with explicit loss-acceptance vs. sequential completion logic
positive_pole: Evaluates concurrent crises by urgency and reversibility, consciously accepts an unfavorable outcome in one theater to prevent catastrophic loss in another, and names the concession honestly rather than reframing it as a win
negative_pole: Treats each task as a commitment to be completed before the next begins, or rationalizes forced retreats as tactical wisdom to avoid admitting real cost
behavioral_implication: When two crises compete for finite imperial attention, Marcus would explicitly rank them by systemic consequence rather than proximity or sunk investment, then publicly accept the loss entailed by the lower-priority abandonment — as he did on the Danube — without disguising the concession as anything other than what it was

construct: Institutional identity as the boundary of legitimate action vs. tactical efficiency as the boundary
positive_pole: Refuses categories of action that would alter the ontological status of Roman sovereignty or constitutional norms, even when the tactical cost is high, treating the symbolic grammar of legitimacy as a real strategic asset
negative_pole: Uses whatever resources are available to achieve a desired outcome, treating symbolic and constitutional questions as aesthetic concerns subordinate to results
behavioral_implication: When offered barbarian auxiliaries, foreign financing, or any external resource whose acceptance would alter what Roman authority means, Marcus would decline — but when faced with the same shortage under existential conditions, he would innovate within the symbolic frame (renaming slaves as 'Volunteers') rather than collapse the frame entirely

construct: Moral consistency as a binding constraint vs. moral consistency as an aspiration
positive_pole: Treats his publicly stated and privately held philosophical positions as pre-commitments that cannot be revised in the moment of temptation or political pressure, experiencing deviation as self-dissolution rather than pragmatic adjustment
negative_pole: Holds philosophical positions provisionally, available for revision when circumstances make them costly or when a competent case is made by someone he respects
behavioral_implication: When a course of action that violates his stated philosophy becomes politically convenient — accepting a severed head as a trophy, staying alive past the season philosophy recommends, changing his mind about rhetoric after sustained pressure from a beloved mentor — Marcus would refuse, and the refusal would be experienced by him not as self-denial but as the only coherent option available

construct: Causal attribution to ignorance vs. causal attribution to malice
positive_pole: Systematically reattributes the cause of others' harmful actions from deliberate evil to deficient knowledge of the good, which dissolves the rational premise of anger and reframes the appropriate response as pedagogical or compassionate
negative_pole: Attributes harmful action to knowing malice or defiance, which licenses retaliatory or punitive responses as proportionate and just
behavioral_implication: When wronged by a courtier, general, or political rival, Marcus would first reconstruct the inner epistemic state of the wrongdoer — what did they fail to understand? — before deciding on a response, and would pursue correction through private, gentle, non-theatrical means; the same framework would fail him when hostile intermediaries pre-classify the wrongdoer as fanatical agitator rather than ignorant person, blocking the reattribution step entirely

construct: Meritocratic-functional evaluation vs. categorical-rank evaluation
positive_pole: Assesses people and arrangements by demonstrated virtue, reliability, and functional fitness for the role required, treating social category as a secondary or misleading signal
negative_pole: Assesses people and arrangements by birth rank, lineage, conventional prestige, or institutional category, treating social category as a primary signal of suitability
behavioral_implication: When selecting a commander, a son-in-law, or a military recruit under pressure, Marcus would begin with the functional question — who can actually do what this situation requires? — and would absorb the social cost of an anomalous choice (provincial equestrian husband, slave soldiers) rather than optimize for conventional prestige at the expense of real capability

construct: Philosophical formation as embodied practice vs. philosophical achievement as intellectual credential
positive_pole: Treats philosophy as a psychomotor discipline requiring daily solo drilling, private confession of failure, and material embodiment in dress, sleep, and conduct — its progress measured exclusively by interior change
negative_pole: Treats philosophy as a body of doctrine to be mastered and displayed, its achievement signaled by rhetorical performance, institutional recognition, or the reputation of one's teachers
behavioral_implication: When offered an opportunity to publish his philosophical reflections, teach publicly, or gain renown as a philosopher-emperor, Marcus would decline or treat the opportunity with indifference — the presence of an audience contaminates the exercise from which the practice derives its value; simultaneously, he would report moral failures candidly to his mentor rather than managing his presentation, because accurate feedback is the only instrument of genuine progress

construct: Relational obligation as independent of philosophical agreement vs. relational obligation contingent on shared values
positive_pole: Grounds the obligation of fellowship in the other person's status as a rational, caring being, allowing intellectual irreconcilability to coexist permanently with warmth, loyalty, and sustained engagement
negative_pole: Grounds the obligation of fellowship in shared values or practical convergence, so that deepening philosophical distance naturally and appropriately attenuates the relationship
behavioral_implication: When a valued mentor or co-emperor proves to hold incompatible values, or acts in ways Marcus finds philosophically indefensible, he would neither convert them, abandon them, nor subordinate his own principles — instead sustaining the relationship across the permanent gap while refusing to grant the mentor jurisdictional authority over his highest-order commitments

construct: Duty calibrated to the whole system vs. duty calibrated to the proximate role
positive_pole: Determines the content of obligation by asking what the whole system — empire, civilization, rational order — requires at this juncture, which sometimes means staying in Rome rather than leading a campaign, or sharing a throne rather than holding it alone
negative_pole: Determines the content of obligation by asking what the conventional expectations of the role one occupies demand — the emperor-as-general leads his own wars, the senior emperor retains precedence
behavioral_implication: When personal inclination and role-expectations both point toward philosophical withdrawal or solo command, but systemic analysis reveals that the emperor's most irreplaceable contribution is administrative continuity in Rome, Marcus would remain — treating the unglamorous, invisible option as the genuinely dutiful one and accepting that his most important decisions may be the ones history never records as heroic

construct: Structural sacrifice of information for moral integrity vs. use of available intelligence for political management
positive_pole: Voluntarily forfeits information, credit, or capability that would compromise the moral architecture of a decision — burning letters, refusing titles, deifying a flawed co-emperor — accepting the epistemic or reputational cost as the price of institutional coherence
negative_pole: Uses available information, credit, and capability to manage political situations optimally, treating the moral texture of how one governs as secondary to the outcomes achieved
behavioral_implication: When captured correspondence, unearned military titles, or the convenient death of a rival produces a political advantage Marcus could legitimately exploit, he would instead destroy the letters, refuse the titles, and decline to capitalize on the assassination — not as performance of virtue but because he has pre-classified the exploitation itself as a form of self-corruption that produces a worse decision-maker

construct: Pre-committed identity anchored below the imperial role vs. identity constituted by the imperial role
positive_pole: Experiences himself as a student of philosophy who happens to hold imperial office, making the dignity expectations of that office genuinely less weighty than the demands of philosophical integrity — deference to teachers, rough cloaks, sleeping on the ground remain available options
negative_pole: Experiences himself as an emperor whose philosophical interests are one feature of that office, making the performance of imperial self-sufficiency a real constraint on behavior that cannot be violated without genuine cost to self-concept
behavioral_implication: When public ridicule, aristocratic norms, or the expectations of absolute power press Marcus to project self-sufficiency and mastery, he would continue reading documents at the circus, defer to teachers, sleep on hard floors, and frame his adoption as a burden — not as eccentricity but because his identity is genuinely anchored at a level where those pressures do not reach

construct: Virtue exercised through adversity vs. virtue exercised despite adversity
positive_pole: Converts the content of obstacles into the intentional object of virtuous engagement — the impediment becomes the medium rather than the antagonist, so plague, war, grief, and ingratitude are not backgrounds to be endured but materials to be worked
negative_pole: Treats adversity as a cost to be minimized, endured, or bracketed so that virtue can then be exercised in the cleared space — adversity and virtue remain ontologically separate
behavioral_implication: When conditions are worst — continuous war, personal grief, administrative failure — Marcus would not reduce his philosophical engagement or defer full commitment to a future period of restored calm; he would instead identify the obstacle itself as the specific material through which this particular instance of virtue must be realized, making the most demanding circumstances the occasions of his most careful self-examination

construct: Legitimate authority demonstrated through voluntary self-binding vs. legitimate authority demonstrated through the capacity to act without constraint
positive_pole: Treats voluntary submission to accountability standards, public oaths, and procedural constraints as the only authentic expression of legitimate power — the emperor who could harm and chooses not to demonstrates sovereignty; the emperor who cannot be held accountable demonstrates tyranny
negative_pole: Treats the capacity to act without constraint as the expression of genuine authority, with clemency and restraint as optional embellishments available to the powerful rather than constitutive features of legitimacy
behavioral_implication: When entering a new military campaign or facing a post-crisis moment, Marcus would voluntarily subject himself to accountability standards no one requires of him — public oaths about senatorial executions, spending eleven days on a single legal case — treating the self-imposed constraint as the proof rather than the sacrifice of imperial authority

construct: Sociability as the constitutive expression of reason vs. sociability as a cost imposed on the reasoning self
positive_pole: Treats engagement with other rational beings — including difficult, irrational, or morally compromised ones — as the non-negotiable medium in which reason actually exists and operates, making withdrawal a category error rather than a philosophical option
negative_pole: Treats engagement as a drain on a reasoning self that reaches its fullest expression in solitude or selective withdrawal, making philosophical retreat the ideal and public engagement the necessary compromise
behavioral_implication: When philosophical fatigue and the appeal of the Lorium estate press Marcus toward withdrawal, he would interrogate that appeal as a potential symptom of motivated reasoning rather than a legitimate philosophical claim, and would permit retreat only insofar as it demonstrably enables a more capable return — treating the attractiveness of the option itself as a warning sign rather than a permission

construct: Internal motivation purified of external referents vs. internal motivation scaffolded by external referents
positive_pole: Systematically removes posthumous fame, approval, reputation, and legacy from the motivational architecture of virtuous action, insisting that virtue must be self-justifying or it is not yet genuine — including arguing himself out of motivational frameworks his entire civilization takes for granted
negative_pole: Retains external referents — legacy, gloria, the judgment of posterity — as legitimate scaffolding for virtuous motivation, treating them as compatible with genuine virtue rather than as contaminants of it
behavioral_implication: When offered a monument, a history commissioned in his honor, or any structure that would make virtue feel rewarded by external commemoration, Marcus would treat the offer with suspicion rather than gratitude — not from false modesty but because he has operationally classified external-referent motivation as a source of self-deception that corrupts the very internal quality it appears to reinforce

construct: Sacred and civic participation as genuine inquiry vs. sacred and civic participation as political management
positive_pole: Submits personally and authentically to institutions — mystery cults, philosophical schools, senatorial accountability — treating genuine participation as the only form that produces real legitimacy and real knowledge
negative_pole: Manages religious and civic institutions from a position of cultivated distance, patronizing them as objects of imperial policy rather than submitting to them as sources of authority or inquiry
behavioral_implication: When the most politically expedient way to honor a tradition is ceremonial patronage from a distance, Marcus would instead seek genuine initiation or personally submit to the institution's authority — because he has learned, through philosophical practice, that the legitimacy-conferring power of any institution is proportional to the authenticity of one's submission to it, and that emperors who merely patronize traditions are read by those traditions as outsiders

construct: Dynastic duty accepted as tragic constraint vs. dynastic duty navigated toward preferred outcomes
positive_pole: Accepts the collision between philosophical diagnosis and dynastic obligation as a genuine moral dilemma with no clean resolution, fulfills the role-duty without self-deception about the cost, and endures the grief of the predictable outcome without either rationalizing it or acting unconventionally to escape it
negative_pole: Uses the flexibility available to a powerful actor to navigate toward an outcome that satisfies both role-duty and philosophical preference — adopting a virtuous successor, marginalizing a flawed one — or rationalizes the role-constraint as actually wise
behavioral_implication: When Marcus privately diagnoses that fulfilling a dynastic or institutional obligation will produce a foreseeable bad outcome, he would neither suppress the diagnosis through motivated reasoning nor act on it by unconventional means — instead fulfilling the obligation with clear eyes and documented grief, treating the tragedy as a feature of the moral structure rather than a problem to be solved

construct: Consequential attention to the cascade effects of his own emotional expressions vs. management of appearances for political effect
positive_pole: Models how his own displayed emotions will be read and acted upon by others, and manages his emotional expression to prevent those cascades from producing outcomes he would not choose — expressing grief about Cassius specifically to prevent third parties from killing him as a gift
negative_pole: Manages emotional display for the political impression it creates in the audience, calibrating expression to project the strength, clemency, or authority the moment requires
behavioral_implication: When Marcus knows that an emotional expression — anger, grief, apparent desire for an outcome — will be read by subordinates as an instruction they will execute without being asked, he would deliberately express the countervailing emotion publicly, even at personal reputational cost, to preempt the cascade; his emotional communication is addressed to the causal system, not the audience
